channel.title: algoatson.github.io
channel.description: A toy char driver frees an object and keeps the pointer. From there it's the usual kernel kata — reclaim the slot with a spray, leak KASLR through the dangling read, then turn the dangling write into a modprobe_path overwrite.

item.title: House of Apple 2 — FSOP after the hooks died
item.description: glibc 2.34 deleted __free_hook and __malloc_hook, and everyone's muscle memory broke. The modern answer is to forge a FILE — but the vtable is validated now. House of Apple 2 slips past that check by abusing a legitimate vtable that dereferences a second, unchecked one.

item.title: the House of Spirit
item.description: Part 7 — instead of corrupting a real chunk, you forge a fake one in memory you already control and trick free() into filing it away. The next malloc hands it right back, so you get malloc to return the stack.

item.title: the poison null byte
item.description: Part 8 — the most innocent-looking heap bug there is: one null byte written one byte too far. It lands on a neighbouring chunk's size field, desyncs the allocator's bookkeeping, and ends with two chunks overlapping the same memory.

item.title: tcache dup
item.description: Part 10 — the fastbin dup's lazier cousin. The tcache had no double-free check at all for two glibc releases, and the one it finally got is a single field you can overwrite. Free a chunk twice, get it twice, poison.

item.title: the House of Corrosion
item.description: Part 14, the finale — for the challenge that gives you no libc leak and barely any allocations. Blow open global_max_fast and the fastbin index arithmetic starts pointing into libc itself, turning "free a chunk of size N" into "write a heap pointer to a libc address". Bootstrap a leak from nothing.

item.title: the House of Einherjar
item.description: Part 11 — one null byte clears a chunk's PREV_INUSE, a forged prev_size says "the previous chunk starts way over there", and free() consolidates backward into a fake chunk you placed anywhere. Arbitrary allocation from a single off-by-one.

item.title: the House of Lore
item.description: Part 12 — the smallbins are doubly-linked and handed out from the tail. Overwrite a smallbin chunk's bk to point at a fake chunk and malloc will walk right into it, returning a chunk on the stack. The unlink check just needs a back-pointer.

item.title: the House of Orange
item.description: Part 9, the capstone — no free() in the binary at all. Force malloc to free the top chunk for you, leak libc from it, then turn the unsorted bin attack into a forged FILE struct whose vtable call drops a shell. Heap meets FSOP.

item.title: the House of Rabbit
item.description: Part 13 — fastbins don't consolidate… until malloc_consolidate sweeps them. Forge a fastbin chunk's size to something enormous, trigger the sweep, and that little chunk becomes a giant free chunk overlapping everything after it. Overlap from almost no primitive.

item.title: the unsorted bin attack
item.description: Part 5 — corrupt one freed chunk's bk and the next malloc writes a libc pointer to an address you choose. You control WHERE, not WHAT — which sounds useless until you aim it at global_max_fast and unlock the whole fastbin.

item.title: unsafe unlink (and the safe-unlink bypass)
item.description: Part 4 — when a free chunk is pulled out of a doubly-linked list, four pointer writes happen. Forge the pointers and one of them lands wherever you want. Then the modern twist: how to pass the "corrupted double-linked list" check that was supposed to kill this.

item.title: fastbin dup
item.description: Part 3 — free a fastchunk twice past glibc's one-line double-free check, get the same chunk handed out twice, then poison its fd to allocate straight onto __malloc_hook. The classic that teaches "allocate where you want".

item.title: the rendering reference
item.description: every block this site knows how to render, in one scroll — listings, the checksec panel, admonitions, spoilers, tables, footnotes. The other seed post is the gentle tour; this one is the exhaustive list, and my regression test.

item.title: the House of Force
item.description: Part 2 — overwrite the top chunk's size with -1 and malloc will hand you a chunk anywhere in the address space. The cleanest "arbitrary allocation" there ever was, and a clean look at why glibc 2.29 killed it.

item.title: the glibc heap, from the chunk up
item.description: Part 1 of the heap series — the only internals that matter for exploitation: chunk anatomy, the size field's flag bits, and the six places a free chunk can live (tcache, fastbins, unsorted, small, large, top). Everything later is corrupting one of these on purpose.

item.title: rizin and radare2 in anger
item.description: The terminal reverse-engineering workflow — open, analyse, navigate, search, patch, and debug a binary without ever leaving the prompt. The commands I actually use, plus where rizin and radare2 diverge.

item.title: a pwntools cookbook
item.description: The pwntools I reach for from memory — the template, tubes, packing, the ELF/libc helpers, format-string and shellcode one-liners, and the CLI subcommands that replace a pile of little scripts. Real snippets, copy-paste shaped.

item.title: SROP — one syscall, every register
item.description: A signal return restores the entire CPU state from a frame on the stack. Forge that frame and a single rt_sigreturn sets rax, rdi, rsi, rdx, rip and rsp at once — the densest gadget in the binary, perfect when you have almost nothing to work with.

item.title: one keybind to checksec everything
item.description: A small workflow I paste into every CTF box — pull every binary's mitigations into one scannable list, run it from a single keybind, and stop re-typing checksec for the hundredth time. Glue, not genius.

item.title: finding gadgets: ROPgadget, ropper, one_gadget, pwntools
item.description: NX means you reuse the code that's already there. Here are the tools that find the pieces, the pwntools layer that assembles them into a chain, and the three structured techniques for when a clean `pop rdi; ret` just isn't in the binary.

item.title: ret2dlresolve — a shell with no libc leak
item.description: When you can't leak libc, make the dynamic linker resolve system() for you. Forge a relocation, a symbol, and a string, point the lazy-binding machinery at them, and _dl_runtime_resolve looks up the address you couldn't.

item.title: pwndbg muscle memory
item.description: The gdb + pwndbg commands I actually type, grouped the way I reach for them rather than the way the docs list them. Heap inspection, the search primitives, breaking under PIE, and the three-command pre-flight before every session.

item.title: seccomp jails, and the holes in them
item.description: A seccomp filter that kills execve but forgets the arch check, the x32 bit, or openat is a jail with the bars painted on. Read the filter, find the gap, and turn a blacklist into open-read-write.

item.title: the first five minutes with an unknown binary
item.description: A fixed triage I run on every new binary before I open a disassembler — identify, enumerate, watch it run. The exact commands, in order, and what each one is actually telling you.

item.title: anti-debug bingo: five checks and how they fall
item.description: A survey of the anti-debug checks you trip over on Linux RE targets — what each one measures, the one line that gives it away, and the cheapest defeat. Folded one per card so you can skim to the one that's biting you right now.

item.title: fuzzing a parser with AFL++, harness first
item.description: A fuzzer is only as good as the harness you point it at. Write a tight persistent-mode target around the parser, build it with instrumentation + ASAN, feed it a real corpus, and let coverage feedback find the inputs you never would. Then triage what falls out.

item.title: two signatures, one nonce: recovering an ECDSA key
item.description: The signing oracle reused a nonce across two messages. That's the whole vulnerability — and it collapses the private key to a couple of modular divisions. The algebra, then the generalisation to merely *biased* nonces and a lattice.

item.title: the stack canary, from the source
item.description: Follow one random value from the kernel's AT_RANDOM, through glibc startup, into fs:0x28, and out to every function prologue. Reading the source explains the canary's quirks — the null low byte, the per-process (not per-call) value — and each quirk is a bypass.

item.title: printf is a write primitive
item.description: A single missing "%s" turns printf into a read-anywhere and write-anywhere primitive. Walk the whole ladder — leak the stack with %p, find your own offset, then bend %n into an arbitrary write — and the mitigations that make it harder but rarely impossible.

item.title: a slab use-after-free, from ioctl to root
item.description: A toy char driver frees an object and keeps the pointer. From there it's the usual kernel kata — reclaim the slot with a spray, leak KASLR through the dangling read, then turn the dangling write into a modprobe_path overwrite.
